era_context: The Old Kingdom of Egypt is in its final year or years of centralized authority. Akkad under Naram-Sin (or possibly his successor Shar-kali-sharri) is approaching final collapse. The Harappan civilization remains in its mature phase. This year marks a critical transition point in the ancient Near East.

events.title: Old Kingdom of Egypt formally ends; First Intermediate Period begins
events.description: The Sixth Dynasty formally ends (traditional date c. 2184 BCE, though some scholars propose c. 2150 BCE). Central pharaonic authority collapses entirely. Egypt fragments into competing regional dynasties, with the Seventh and Eighth Dynasties claiming nominal authority from Memphis while Upper Egyptian nomarchs establish independent kingdoms. This marks the beginning of the First Intermediate Period (c. 2181–2055 BCE), a 130-year period of political fragmentation and reduced monumental construction.
key_figures: Pepi II (Pharaoh, Sixth Dynasty; traditional dates c. 2278–2184 BCE)
Merenre II (Pharaoh, Sixth Dynasty; traditional dates c. 2184–2181 BCE)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence of reduced monument construction and administrative activity

events.certainty_note: The end of the Old Kingdom is well-attested; the precise date is debated (±20 years). Traditional chronology places it c. 2184 BCE, but some scholars propose c. 2150 BCE or c. 2100 BCE.
cross_references: Follows from Old Kingdom decline (-2134 onwards)
Leads to First Intermediate Period fragmentation (-2130 to -2055)

events.title: Akkad Empire in final crisis; Naram-Sin dies; succession disputed
events.description: Naram-Sin dies (traditional date c. 2218 BCE, though some scholars propose c. 2200 BCE). His successor, Shar-kali-sharri (r. c. 2217–2193 BCE in traditional chronology), inherits an empire in severe crisis. Gutian invasions intensify, and internal rebellion spreads. The succession is disputed, and the empire's administrative apparatus is collapsing. This marks the beginning of the end for Akkadian hegemony; the empire will formally collapse within 20–30 years.
events.certainty_note: The death of Naram-Sin and succession of Shar-kali-sharri are well-attested; precise dating is uncertain (±10–20 years). The date 2131 BCE is assigned by convention; the actual year may differ by a decade or more.
cross_references: Part of Akkad Empire decline (c. 2200–2154 BCE)
Leads to Gutian conquest and Akkad collapse (-2154 onwards)

events.title: Harappan civilization at peak of urban development; extensive trade networks active
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization continues in its mature phase, with major cities functioning as centers of trade, craft production, and administration. Trade goods (lapis lazuli, carnelian, timber) are exchanged with Mesopotamia, the Persian Gulf, and Central Asia. The civilization's standardized weights, measures, and seals indicate organized commerce. Despite the political turmoil in Egypt and Mesopotamia, Harappan trade networks remain resilient.

sources.name: Archaeological excavations at Mohenjo-daro, Harappa, and other Indus sites
Mesopotamian cuneiform references to trade with Meluhha

events.certainty_note: The existence and characteristics of the Harappan civilization in this period are well-established through archaeology. Absolute dating relies on radiocarbon and stratigraphic correlation.
cross_references: Contemporary with Egyptian and Akkadian political crises
Part of Harappan mature phase (c. 2600–1900 BCE)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Old Kingdom's end is debated; some scholars place it earlier (c. 2150 BCE) or later (c. 2100 BCE). The traditional date of c. 2184 BCE is based on Manetho's regnal lengths, which are disputed. The death of Naram-Sin and the timing of Akkad's collapse are also debated; some scholars propose the empire remained relatively stable until c. 2154 BCE, while others suggest earlier collapse. The Harappan civilization's resilience during this period is sometimes questioned; some scholars argue that trade disruptions occurred earlier than traditionally assumed.
historiographic_note: This year marks a critical transition in the ancient Near East, but it is poorly documented in contemporary sources. Egyptian records are fragmentary and elite-focused. Mesopotamian sources concentrate on Akkadian imperial narratives and later Sumerian retrospectives. The Indus Valley Civilization's undeciphered script prevents direct historical narrative. Chronological precision is limited to ±10–20 years. The vast majority of human societies left no written records, making global history invisible.

graph_edges.note: Old Kingdom collapse leads to First Intermediate Period
Akkad's final crisis leads to Gutian conquest and collapse
Egyptian and Akkadian political crises occur simultaneously
